chapters.title: See the Default Reaction Before It Runs You

summaryBullets: Recognizing the default reaction before it becomes the decision creates clearer judgment.
Clear thinking begins when people notice the impulse that wants to act first.
This matters because bad decisions often happen before the conscious story catches up.
A common mistake is mistaking the first emotional urge for a sound conclusion.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the body is already moving toward a decision before the reasoning is clear.
A practical starting move is to label the first impulse, pause before committing, and write the real objective.
keyTakeaways: Recognizing the default reaction before it becomes the decision creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: label the first impulse
pause before committing
write the real objective
choose the response after the emotion settles

chapters.title: Create Space Between Event and Response

summaryBullets: Building a small gap between the trigger and the next move creates clearer judgment.
Better judgment needs enough space for perspective and choice.
This matters because a short gap often prevents a long chain of avoidable damage.
A common mistake is reacting in the same emotional frame that the event created.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the conversation speeds up and options shrink because nobody paused.
A practical starting move is to take one deliberate pause, ask what matters most here, and separate the trigger from the outcome you want.
keyTakeaways: Building a small gap between the trigger and the next move creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: take one deliberate pause
ask what matters most here
separate the trigger from the outcome you want
use a short rule before you respond
